guides.title: Healing
guides.content:
Healing
is the act of recovering a unit's current
health
, which includes various methods such as lifesteal, health regen, and item effects.
Healing Amp
[
edit
|
edit source
]
Healing Amp, also known as Healing Effectiveness, is a
stat
which increases the amount of healing a hero does with their
abilities
and
items
. This affects both healing to themselves and to their allies. Healing Amp affects healing-over-time buffs, such as
Healing Rite
's active effect.
This
stat
is represented as a percentage. For example, a 25% gain would increase a heal of 100 to 125.
Healing Reduction
[
edit
|
edit source
]
Healing Reduction is a negative effect which reduces the effectiveness of healing on the unit it is applied to.
Healing Reduction is represented as a percentage and stacks multiplicatively, meaning each additional source has diminishing returns.
If a player is hit by the same source of Healing Reduction before its effect has worn off (e.g. two or more enemy players with Healbane), only the first source is applied.
Calculation
[
edit
|
edit source
]
Calculating Healing Reduction
[
edit
|
edit source
]
Multiple sources of Healing Reduction stack
multiplicatively
:
Total Reduction
=
1
−
(
1
−
R
1
)
×
(
1
−
R
2
)
…
Example:
If two sources of Healing Reduction are applied, one providing
40%
and another providing
40%
:
Convert percentages to decimals: 0.40 and 0.40.
Subtract from 1: (1 - 0.40) =
0.60
and (1 - 0.40) =
0.60
.
Multiply the remainders: 0.60 × 0.60 =
0.36
.
Subtract from 1: 1 - 0.36 =
0.64
.
Total Healing Reduction is
64%
, not 80%.
Calculating Final Healing
[
edit
|
edit source
]
Final Healing
=
Initial Healing
×
(
1
−
Total Reduction
)
×
(
1
+
Healing Amp
)
Example:
A heal of
200 HP
is applied to a target with
64%
Total Healing Reduction. The healer has
25%
Healing Amp.
Apply Reduction: 200 × (1 - 0.64) = 200 × 0.36 =
72 HP
.
Apply Amp: 72 × (1 + 0.25) = 72 × 1.25 =
90 HP
.
Final Healing received is
90 HP
.
Note: Healing from self-damage sources such as
Victor
's
Pain Battery
and
Aura of Suffering
does not appear in the endgame hero stats screen, but it does appear in the healing sources graph.
Sources of direct healing
[
edit
|
edit source
]
Killing a
Medic Trooper
drops a Medic Pack that heals nearby players based on the following formula:
Heal = (14% of hero's missing
HP
+ 150 HP + 3 HP per minute) / Number of nearby heroes
Heroes
[
edit
|
edit source
]
Items
[
edit
|
edit source
]
Sources of healing reduction
[
edit
|
edit source
]
Heroes
[
edit
|
edit source
]
Items
[
edit
|
edit source
]
Cheat Death
applies
-60%
healing reduction to its user for 4.5 seconds (the same duration as the invulnerability).
See also
[
edit
|
edit source
]
Bullet Lifesteal
Spirit Lifesteal
Health Regen

guides.title: Health
guides.content:
Health
measures the amount of
damage
that can be taken before
dying
, and is fundamental to all
heroes
and units. Units and players with health are allowed to interact with the game, and consequently when your health drops to zero you are disabled from playing until respawning.
Maximum Health
[
edit
|
edit source
]
Kelvin's starting base health at
0
Max health is calculated as follows:
Max HP
=
(
Base HP
+
Boons Earned
×
HP per Boon
)
×
(
1
+
Vitality Bonuses
)
+
Bonus HP
Where:
Base HP
is the hero's starting health value.
Boons Earned
is the number of
Boons
the hero has earned, multiplied by the hero's hero-specific HP gained per Boon.
Vitality Bonuses
are the percentage-based health increases from
Vitality
items, starting at 8% and increasing with further Vitality investment.
Bonus HP
is flat health added from items, map pickups, and other sources, applied after the Vitality multiplier.
Note that the max health presented in-game is rounded up to the nearest integer.
Base Health
[
edit
|
edit source
]
Kelvin's base health at
20.0k
Base health is the sum of starting base health and added base health from boons. It is scaled by the inherent increased base health stats of Vitality items. Different heroes have different amounts of starting base health and added base health from boons.
Values referenced from
Hero Attributes Table
.
Bonus Health
[
edit
|
edit source
]
Survival power-up located in the training map
Bonus health comes from a variety of sources:
Items are the most common source of added bonus health. Bonus health can be found on all three item types, but Vitality items tend to provide the largest amounts.
Golden statues
occasionally drop small but permanent added bonus health buffs:
+20
before 10 minutes,
+30
between 10 and 30 minutes, or
+40
after 30 minutes.
The
Survival Powerup
immediately grants
+200
to
+700
bonus health (scales as game goes on), but only for 160 seconds or until death.
The
Rejuvenator
immediately grants
+200
bonus health, but only for 4 minutes or until death.
Bonus Health is not scaled by increased base health. Bonus health is added after the increased base health multiplier has been applied to base health.
Miscellaneous Health Modifiers
[
edit
|
edit source
]
With the purchase of
Glass Cannon
, the hero's max health is reduced by -15% after the above calculations.
Example
[
edit
|
edit source
]
Kelvin
begins the game with 600 Base HP. If Kelvin purchases two tier 1 vitality items, one of which grants 100 bonus health, his max health becomes:
Max HP
=
600
×
(
1
+
0.10
+
0.10
)
+
100
=
820
If Kelvin then earns a power increase, his base health increases by 50, and his max health becomes:
Max HP
=
(
600
+
50
)
×
(
1
+
0.10
+
0.10
)
+
100
=
880
Regaining Health
[
edit
|
edit source
]
Main articles:
Healing
,
Health Regen
From the start of the match, heroes will regenerate health slowly over time, typically 2 HP per second. This rate can be increased by purchasing items such as
Extra Regen
and
Fortitude
. Health can be regained quickly by returning to the spawn room of the team's base. Various hero abilities can heal, such as Dynamo's
Rejuvenating Aurora
. Sources of
spirit
and
bullet lifesteal
grant heroes the opportunity to regain health by dealing the respective damage type.
Health Bars
[
edit
|
edit source
]
A 600 HP Kelvin right next to a 1300 HP Kelvin
Health Bars
are visual indicators of a hero's current health, located just above each hero. Health bars consist of filled rectangles, arranged in rows of up to ten. Enemy heroes are displayed with orange rectangles, while allied heroes use an off-white color. As the hero loses health, the rectangles deplete horizontally. Each full rectangle represents 100 health, and each full row represents 1000 health.
The small yellow and blue bars above the health bar represent bullet
shields
and spirit shields, respectively.
Alternate health bar
[
edit
|
edit source
]
The alternate health bar is an experimental bar that can be tested by players. This bar always has the same size, and the health count is displayed as markings instead of rectangles. One marking represents 100 health, and a line represents 500 health (from left to right).
This health bar also indicates when other players have ultimates ready, represented by an eye icon that fills up when the ultimate is recharged. Shields are represented by a purple bar.
The alternate health bar can be enabled with the console command
citadel_unit_status_use_new true
. The command can be added with a
+
to the game launch options on Steam to be enabled on startup.
Sources
[
edit
|
edit source
]
All
vitality items
provide bonus health based on the player's total investment in the category.
The following items explicitly grant increased bonus health:
Map spawns
[
edit
|
edit source
]
Golden Statues
can provide a permanent increase: +
20
Health before 10 minutes, +
30
Health before 30 minutes, or +
40
Health after 30 minutes.
Powerup
runes can provide a temporary Survival buff that provides +
200
to +
700
Bonus Health for 160 seconds (scaling with game time from 5 to 40 minutes).
Mid-Boss
can provide a temporary Rejuvenator Credit buff that provides +200 Bonus Health for four minutes or until death, whichever comes first.

guides.title: Heroes
guides.content:
Heroes
Attributes Table
Release Date
Heroes
are the playable characters of
Deadlock
.
There are currently
38
heroes available, with more to be released as the game develops.
Overview
[
edit
|
edit source
]
Heroes come in a wide variety in Deadlock, each with their own specialisations and playstyles. Each hero wields their own unique primary weapon which has its own name, stats and weapon type. Weapons are affected by
Weapon
Class
Items
and are the primary means of dealing damage for most heroes. Some weapons possess an alternate fire mode activated with
RMB
.
Besides their weapons, heroes also possess 4 unique abilities with a variety of effects. Some abilities are completely passive, and will always be active regardless (such as
Abrams
'
Infernal Resilience
), though most are simply activated with their respective bindings (
1
,
2
,
3
,
4
by default). Abilities come with up to 3 upgrades, which can be purchased with an increasing number of
Ability Points
. Abilities most often scale with
Spirit Power
.
Heroes have set base stats that they can improve through
leveling up
or purchasing
items
. They also have additional
Power Increases
, gained at specific levels which grant bonuses to specific stats. Heroes can hold up to 12 individual items at a time, so long as they conform to their categories and
Extra Slots
. Some hero stats are also affected by
Spirit Power
, referred to as Spirit Scaling.
38
heroes are available in the current alpha build, with additional characters confirmed for future releases.
Heroes
[
edit
|
edit source
]
Hero Labs
[
edit
|
edit source
]
Hero Labs
was an alternative game mode where players could test unfinished heroes.
The following Heroes were only accessible in Hero Labs, before the mode was discontinued.
Unused Heroes
[
edit
|
edit source
]
There are a number of heroes in
Deadlock
that can only be accessed using
console commands
, or that were removed from the game's code. Some of them are older heroes inherited from
Neon Prime
, while others were added to the game more recently.
Boho
The Boss
Cadence
Druid
Fortuna
Graf
Gunslinger
Kali
Rutger
Skyrunner
Swan
Thumper
Tokamak
Vandal
Trivia
[
edit
|
edit source
]
At one point in early development, the Heroes were called
Fates
.
[
1
]
The Hero backstories were previously called
"A Fate's Tale"
.
In the French localization, the Heroes are called
Occultistes
.
References
[
edit
|
edit source
]
↑
2024-08-14
Yoshi
; Deadlock Discord -
"We had them called The Fates at some point earlier on, but we stuck with heroes for now. We'll probably rename them in the future."
{{#seo:
|description=Deadlock's 38 Heroes come in a wide variety, each with their own specialisations and playstyles. Each hero wields their own unique primary weapon which has its own name, stats and weapon type. Weapons are affected by
Weapon
Class
Items
and are the primary means of dealing damage for most heroes. Besides their weapons, heroes wield strange and spiritual abilities that may inflict damage, apply buffs, or otherwise affect the outcome of a match.
|keywords=Deadlock, Characters, Deadlock characters, Heroes, Hero, Roster, MOBA, New heroes, Shooter, Game, Valve
|image=DeadlockWikiSEO.png}}

guides.title: Hideout
guides.content:
The main area of the Hideout.
The Hideout
is a personal, interactive hub that functions as the main menu and social area in
Deadlock
. Within this space, players can freely move around, prepare for matches, and invite friends to join them. Various terminals located throughout the Hideout provide access to the game's core menus, including Play, Watch, and Learn.
For training, the Hideout includes a dedicated practice range called The Atrium, designed for testing hero movement and abilities without a loading screen. Players can access the more extensive Sandbox mode through a
Teleporter
found within this practice area.
Overview
[
edit
|
edit source
]
The Hideout serves as an immersive replacement for traditional 2D menus, with players loading into this space upon launching the game. The environment is a grand, multi-level manor room with an opulent, gothic atmosphere, featuring a large fireplace, an extensive library, and towering windows that offer a view of
The Cursed Apple
at dusk. This polished main hall stands in contrast to the adjoining Atrium, which has a more raw, industrial design.
Layout
[
edit
|
edit source
]
Players spawn in the center of the main hall.
Directly in front is the
Play Terminal
, which serves as the room's focal point.
To the right is the
Learn to Play Terminal
, situated in a cozy library nook by a fireplace.
To the left, a path leads towards the
Shop & Atrium
.
In the back-right corner of the room is a separate viewing lounge that houses the
Watch Terminal
.
A large, ornate staircase dominates the back of the room, leading to an upper balcony. The left side of the balcony is a furnished trophy room, while the right side is an undeveloped storage area filled with crates and sandbags.
Functional Areas
[
edit
|
edit source
]
The main functionalities of the game are accessed through distinct stations scattered around the room. Interacting with a terminal opens a full-screen UI menu.
Play Terminal
[
edit
|
edit source
]
Located in the center of the room, the Play Terminal is the primary station for matchmaking. It appears as a circular table surrounded by a glowing green summoning circle. Interacting with it opens the "Select Play Mode" menu, which includes options for:
Deadlock:
Standard player-vs-player matches.
Street Brawl
:
Quick-paced 4v4 battle.
Bot Match:
Play against AI bots, either privately or with other players.
Sandbox Mode:
A comprehensive training mode with advanced tools.
Explore NYC:
A mode to freely explore the map.
Custom Match:
Create private lobbies with custom rules.
Learn to Play Terminal
[
edit
|
edit source
]
Situated in the library area to the right of the main hall, this terminal is a book resting on a stand in front of an armchair. Interacting with it provides access to the game's Basic Tutorial. This is also accessible from the Play Terminal for new players.
Watch Terminal
[
edit
|
edit source
]
Found in a back room, the Watch Terminal consists of a vintage film projector aimed at a screen. This station is used to access the replay and spectator system, allowing players to watch live matches or search for a specific match by its ID.
News Terminal
[
edit
|
edit source
]
The news terminal is a corner under the stairs with newspapers, magazines and a mystic personal computer powered by candles and using an Ouija planchette as a mouse. Interacting with the terminal shows news about recent updates published on Steam.
Vote Terminal
[
edit
|
edit source
]
Found on a table near the entrance to the Shop & Practice Space, the Vote Terminal is a wooden ballot box that allowed the community to influence which Hero to be added next. Interacting with it opened the "Who's Next?" menu, where players were able to cast votes for which hero they would like to see released next. Players earned one vote for each match they play. A timer on the UI indicated when the next hero unlock was scheduled to occur. Voting added stickers to the walls:
Six New Heroes
:
"Eye Voted" stickers
Old Gods, New Blood
:
Stickers depicting the face of the hero voted for
Shop & Atrium
[
edit
|
edit source
]
The antechamber containing The Curiosity Shop.
To the left of the main hall is an open doorway leading to a two-part training area.
The Curiosity Shop
[
edit
|
edit source
]
The antechamber to the practice range features a fully-stocked counter for
The Curiosity Shop
. While players can access the item shop at any time with a dedicated keybind.
The Atrium
[
edit
|
edit source
]
The main practice area is a large, industrial-looking room with scaffolding and graffiti. It is designed for basic ability and damage testing.
Bots:
The range contains one friendly and one enemy bot. These bots are static and do not perform any actions, serving as simple targets.
Training Tools:
A large, red-buttoned machine allows players to toggle fast cooldowns and infinite stamina by meleeing it.
When in a party, a crystal appears that allows players to swap teams.
Teleporter to Sandbox:
At the back of the range is a
teleporter
that allows players to instantly load into the full Sandbox mode for more advanced training options.
Animals:
The Atrium has a cat and a flying owl. The cat chases the ball, as well as
Calico
in
Ava
's form, the laser pointer from
Vindicta
's
rifle
,
The Doorman
's
Call Bell
, and
Viscous
in
Goo Ball
form.
Kicking the ball into the teleporter 3 times will spawn a rabbit. The rabbit has the same behavior as the cat. If the cat and the rabbit come into proximity of each other, and aren't already chasing one of the aforementioned objects, the cat will chase the rabbit around the room.
Shooting the owl makes it disappear and spawns a new owl, as well as a second one, increasing the number of owls by one every time all owls are shot. It also shows a counter of how many owls were shot by the player in the current session.
Customization
[
edit
|
edit source
]
Winter 2025 Souvenir
The Hideout is planned to be customizable with items unlocked by the player. The first two unlockables are:
A snow globe above the fireplace for players who engaged in the
Winter 2024
event challenges.
A Merry Midtown Mayhem Souvenir for players who participated in the
Winter 2025
event.
Status
[
edit
|
edit source
]
The Hideout has a unique
Rich Presence
status for each hero that appears for friends and players in a party in the main menu, as well as on the Steam friends menu.
Gallery
[
edit
|
edit source
]
Hideout window view (title screen)
Trivia
[
edit
|
edit source
]
Shadowy silhouettes of
Billy
,
The Doorman
,
Drifter
,
Mina
,
Paige
, and
Victor
could be seen throughout the Hideout before their release during the
Six New Heroes
update. Similarly, shadowy silhouettes of
Apollo
,
Celeste
,
Graves
,
Rem
,
Silver
and
Venator
could be seen throughout the Hideout before their release during the
Old Gods, New Blood
update.
On
20th Janurary 2026
, the
Old Gods, New Blood
update's heroes were teased with various objects scattered around the Hideout (such as
Apollo
's sword,
Rem
's pillow and
Venator
's thurible).
The clock, located above the elevator in the hideout, displays the player's local system time while playing solo. However, upon joining a party, it switches to reflect the local time in Seattle, Washington (PST/PDT), the location of Valve's headquarters. The clock also flashes and dings every 60 seconds. On the hour, it also chimes to match the current hour.
See Also
[
edit
|
edit source
]
The Cursed Apple

guides.title: Jump pad
guides.content:
Jump Pads
, also known as
Bounce Pads
,
Launch Pads
or
Air Vents
, are structures on the
Map
that launch a player in the air in a specific direction, indicated by the wind above the pad.
Overview
[
edit
|
edit source
]
The landing location is usually indicated by a white graffiti on the ground. The player can air strafe by looking and moving to a different direction mid-jump.
Taking damage in the first 0.6s after using a Jump Pad will stun the player until 0.2s after landing, apply a 30% slow for 2s, and deal 15% Max Health damage.
Holliday
has the ability to place
Bounce Pads
. Holliday's pads launch the player upwards in the direction they were moving on the ground.

guides.title: Mechanics
guides.content:
Pocket
attacking enemies from above
An item build
Mechanics
in
Deadlock
are the details, particulars, and mechanisms by which a player interacts with the game. This includes the objectives, controls, timing, map, and priorities to consider while playing in order to achieve victory.
Mechanics are generally broken into three categories:
Unit mechanics
The statistics, controls, and statuses of the units of
Deadlock
, including the player's Hero, the marching
Troopers
, and lane
Structures
.
Gameplay mechanics
The mechanics of interacting with the game, how the player gains
Souls
, victory/defeat conditions, damage, healing, and death.
Map mechanics
The locations and entities of the game map and how they interact with players.
Overview
[
edit
|
edit source
]
The overall objective of
Deadlock
is to destroy the enemy team's
Patron
. This is done over the course of the game, where two teams battle across three lanes, navigating the map using
movement
tools such as fans,
ropes
, and
Ziplines
. Each lane contains a
Guardians
and a
Walkers
that a team will need to destroy to progress to and destroy the
Patron
.
Teams battle AI controlled Troopers, that release Souls when killed. Souls are used to
Level-up
and buy
items
.
Gameplay Mechanics
[
edit
|
edit source
]
Offense and Defense
[
edit
|
edit source
]
Weapon Attacks
Every Hero has a weapon that fires a ranged projectile that allows them to engage in ranged combat to deal damage to other Heroes and units.
Melee Attacks
Main article:
Melee Attack
Every Hero may also perform a
melee
attack (Default key:
Q
) to strike at point-blank range. Players can perform either a light or heavy melee attack. Light melee attacks are fast but deal low damage, heavy melee attacks take longer to charge, but have greater damage and range.
Parry
Parries
(Default key:
F
) are a defensive move that is available to every Hero, in which they can block a melee hit from an enemy. On a successful parry, the enemy is stunned and takes 20% more damage from all sources for 2.75s. The parry goes on cooldown once it's used, but is instantly refunded on a successful parry. Troopers and Guardians can also be parried.
Crouch
Crouching (Default key:
Ctrl
) can help avoid enemy fire by making the player smaller and able to hide behind objects.
Hero Abilities
Every Hero has 4 unique abilities, which they can unlock for use by spending
ability unlocks
. Abilities provide tools for Heroes to enhance their playstyle in several different ways, such as dealing damage, empowering themselves or allied Heroes, weakening enemies, or assisting in traversal.
Movement
[
edit
|
edit source
]
Main article:
Movement
Double jump
Heroes can perform a
double jump
by jumping (Default key:
Space
) while in mid air, which cost 1
Stamina
. By default, Heroes can perform a single
double jump
in the air.
Dash and Air Dash
Pressing the dash button (Default key:
Shift
) performs a quick evasive maneuver, which costs 1
Stamina
. It can be used to evade enemy attacks, escape fights, or chase enemies.
Pressing dash while in the air performs an
air dash
which works exactly like a regular dash, except it has less slowdown at the end of the dash. Without items, Heroes can perform a single
air dash
while in the air.
Most Heroes have unique dash speeds, this can be viewed in their Vitality stats.
Dash Jump
A
Dash jump
is a fast long jump, which can be performed during a dash and costs 1
Stamina
, for a total of 2 including the Stamina spent
dashing
.
The timing of a
dash jump
is visualized by the Stamina bar turning blue while
dashing.
While the Stamina bar is blue, the player can press jump to successfully
dash jump
.
If players miss the timing of a
dash jump
by pressing
Space
too early or too late, the Hero will continue to dash and players will not be able to attempt the
dash jump
again until the initial dash is completed.
Slide
Heroes can slide by holding crouch while moving faster than 9.6m/s (such as with high move/sprint speed, after dashes, etc) or while going down slopes, preserving momentum and giving infinite ammo for the duration of the slide. This can improve overall DPS by increasing the time before needing to reload when fighting around stairs or slopes, such as the ones seen in every lane next to the
Guardian
.
Down Dash
Heroes can quickly dash downwards while in mid air by pressing the crouch button twice, spending 0.5
Stamina
.
Wall Jump
Heroes can perform a
wall jump
by jumping in mid air next to a wall while inputting the direction away from the wall. The first wall jump costs no
Stamina
, while subsequent wall jumps cost 0.5
Stamina
.
Wall Slide
Heroes can slide along walls while in the air, slowing their descent and limiting their max falling speed to -600 u/s.
Mantle
Heroes can
mantle
, i.e. climb up obstacles. To mantle, the player must hold the forward key and look in the direction they wish to mantle in while jumping.
Mantle Glide
Heroes can perform a
mantle glide
by holding the crouch button during a mantle. This will add a speed boost upon the mantle finishing.
General Strategy
[
edit
|
edit source
]
Laning
Laning is the process of fighting in the lane, collecting
Souls
from
Troopers
, and pushing the lane to the enemy
Patron
.
There are 3 lanes: York (Yellow), Broadway (Blue), and Park (Green; also referred to as Greenwich). Any part of a lane controlled by the opposing team will be red on the
Minimap
.
The
laning phase
refers to the early game where players organize in lanes to gain strength (see: "
Strategy
").
Ganking
Ganking is the act of moving from one lane to another to help kill vulnerable enemy Heroes.
Farming
Farming is the process of focusing on gaining souls and buffs to increase your Hero's strength and buy more items. Farming typically consists of killing
Denizen
camps and destroying breakables such as
Crates
,
Golden Statues
, and
Sinner's Sacrifice
machines.
Items
[
edit
|
edit source
]
Main article:
Items
All
Items
give passive bonuses and some of them have an Active ability that Hero can use on command. Items can be purchased from the
The Curiosity Shop
for
Souls
.
Comeback Mechanics
[
edit
|
edit source
]
When a team is behind on
Souls
, there are some mechanics that come into play to allow the losing team to catch up.
Killing an enemy Hero will award more Souls if the player's team is behind.
Killing an enemy Hero that has a Kill Streak will reward a "Bounty" of extra Souls.
The losing team gains up to 30% more souls from troopers, neutrals, vaults, and objectives, based on how behind they are (peaks at 20% max net worth difference). The first 3k in net worth difference is ignored.
The
Soul Urn
drop-off location is located in the side lanes if teams are even, but located on the losing team's side if they have significantly less total souls (10% or more difference).
Map Mechanics
[
edit
|
edit source
]
Main article:
The Cursed Apple
The map is where all of the fighting in
Deadlock
takes place. Known as "The Cursed Apple" and based on Midtown and Lower Manhattan in New York City, the map contains all of the unique features of
Deadlock's
gameplay.
Minimap
[
edit
|
edit source
]
Main article:
Minimap
The Minimap provides players with a view of the battle at large, providing live information regarding relevant match information.
The Minimap provides specific information of the location of Camps, friendly and enemy Troopers, Shops, Buffs, and enemy Heroes (under certain conditions).
The Minimap should be checked often to give players insight on the status of their own team, lane status, enemy positions, and camp locations.
Structures and Units
[
edit
|
edit source
]
Interactive Objects
[
edit
|
edit source
]
Temporary Buffs
[
edit
|
edit source
]
Main article:
Powerups
Temporary buffs spawn on the bridges overlooking the Soul Urn locations and begin at the 5 minute mark and then every 5 minutes thereafter. Each buff lasts for 160 seconds after being destroyed with a Heavy
Melee Attack
. The strength of each buff progressively increases from the 5 minute mark to the 40 minute mark.
There are four types of buffs:
Casting Buff:
Grants temporary cooldown reduction and spirit power.
Starts at +15
Spirit Power
and +12% Cooldown Reduction
Ends at +40 Spirit Power and +25% Cooldown Reduction
Duration: 160s
Gun Buff:
Grants temporary magazine size and fire rate.
Starts at +12%
Fire Rate
and +20%
Ammo
Ends at +35% Fire Rate and +60% Ammo
Duration: 160s
Movement Buff:
Grants temporary extra stamina and sprint speed.
Starts at +1
Stamina
, +1.5m/s
Sprint Speed
, +20% Stamina Regeneration Rate, and +40% Zipline Boost
Ends at +4 Stamina, +4m/s Sprint Speed, +50% Stamina Regeneration Rate, and +80% Zipline Boost
Duration: 160s
Survival Buff:
Grants temporary health and health regen.
Starts at +200
Bonus Health
and +0.5%
Max Health Regen
Ends at +700 Bonus Health and +1.5% Max Health Regen
Duration: 160s

guides.title: Melee Attack
guides.content:
Seven
performing a heavy melee attack.
Every hero can perform a
melee attack
to quickly finish off an enemy or do damage while reloading.
Melee damage
scales with levels and with certain
items
like
Melee Lifesteal
and
Lifestrike
. A light melee attack can be charged up to a heavy melee attack to lunge forward without using up any of the player's
Stamina
and do more damage; however, the amount a player can turn is reduced during the attack animation. Compared to a normal
dash
using one Stamina bar, a heavy melee attack lunge travels the player approximately half of the distance. Charging the melee attack can also be done in the air. The default keybind for melee attacks is
Q
. Pressing
Q
triggers a light melee attack, while holding initiates a heavy melee attack.
The cooldown for a heavy melee is 1 second, if you miss your heavy melee it will increase to 1.3 seconds.
Overview
[
edit
|
edit source
]
See
Melee Damage
for a list of all heroes' base melee damage and scaling.
Melee attack damage benefits from 50% of all
Weapon Damage
buffs.
Last hitting a Trooper with a melee attack will prevent its
Soul
Orb from appearing and give player the full amount of Souls they would have gotten if they shot the Trooper and its orb. Players can perform melee attacks to secure orbs, though they can only do so after a short delay when denying enemy Soul Orbs.
Melee attacks that hit a player climbing an object, such as a ledge or
rope
, will cause an 80% slowdown that ramps down to 20% over 2 seconds. Melee attacks that hit a player carrying the
Soul Urn
will force them to drop the urn.
Melee attacks are the only way to interact with the
Sinner's Sacrifice
and will cause 80 retaliation damage (regardless of whether a Light or Heavy melee was used).
Heavy melee attack is the only way to get the
Buffs
, claim
Rejuvenator
or loot the bag of unsecured
Souls
dropped by a killed hero.
Performing a melee attack will decrease the player's speed shortly after. The amount of speed decreased varies between heroes, and additionally varies depending on if a light or heavy melee is used. The speed decrease can be mitigated through items that give resistance to
movement slow
effects, such as
Fleetfoot
and
Enduring Speed
.
Melee attacks will interrupt
reloading
when performed. Light melee attacks (with the exception of
Billy
's) instantly freeze the reload timer on use, while heavy melee attacks will instead freeze the reload timer once the attacking hero begins lunging. Reloading resumes once the melee animation has completed.
Parry
[
edit
|
edit source
]
Parries (Default key:
F
) are a defensive move that is available to every Hero.
After performing a parry, a Hero briefly enters
a parry state
. The parry animation lasts 1 second, during which any incoming melee attack will be parried for the first 0.75 seconds. During the
parry state
, the player can fully absorb melee attacks and apply the following status effects to the melee attackers: stunning the attacker for 2.75 seconds (cannot be reduced by debuff resistance), preventing incoming melee damage, and making the attacker susceptible to increased damage in return (+25% to all damage sources, not just the individual who parried).
A successful parry has a brief 0.3 second window exiting the parry animation in which the player will not be able to perform anything, after which any action (including another parry) can be performed. Holding the parry key allows the player to parry multiple heroes. An unsuccessful parry has a cooldown of 4.5 seconds before another parry can be performed again.
Debuff Resist does not impact the duration of those status effects from being parried. However,
Unstoppable
(and abilities that grant the same effect) does prevent being stunned by a successful parry.
Counterspell
and
Rebuttal
give additional effects to parries, with Rebuttal also decreasing parry cooldown to 2.5 seconds.
You can parry
Troopers
(blocks damage but doesn't stun them) and
Guardians
(blocks damage and prevents them from attacking for a short period; parried Guardian will not attempt another melee attack for a short while). You cannot normally parry
Walkers
or most abilities, with an exception of
Viscous
'
Puddle Punch
.
Heavy Melee Cancel
[
edit
|
edit source
]
Main article:
Heavy Melee Cancel
Heavy Melee Cancel
is a technique that allows you to interrupt a Heavy Melee attack by using specific
Active Items
or
Abilities
. This is particularly useful for melee-focused builds, as it can bait enemies into wasting their
Parry
.
https://deadlock.wiki/File:Heavy_Melee_Cancel.mp4
Baiting Heavy Melee by canceling it with
Fleetfoot
.

guides.title: Minimap
guides.content:
“Let's get started, shall we?”
This article is a
stub
. You can help the Deadlock Wiki by expanding it.
The
Minimap
is a simplified depiction of the
map
from above. It is used in a match to navigate the map, see the location of teammates, and to see any enemy players within the player's or players' teammates line of sight.
In spectator mode, the Minimap has
The Archmother
side as North and
The Hidden King
side as South by default.
Overview
[
edit
|
edit source
]
Reveal Duration
[
edit
|
edit source
]
When revealed on the minimap, the player remains visible for approximately
2 seconds
after the revealing action ends.
Things that reveal the player to enemies on the minimap
[
edit
|
edit source
]
Shooting enemy
Troopers
.
Being targeted or shot at by enemy Troopers.
Shooting at enemy defensive structures (
Guardians
,
Walkers
,
Shrines
,
Patron
).
This includes shooting them from an angle where the structure cannot see or shoot back. The act of shooting at the structure is what causes the player to appear on the enemy minimap, regardless of location.
Being targeted or shot at by enemy defensive structures.
Being within an enemy hero's line of sight, within approximately
50–60 meters
. Note that this is based on the heroes' line of sight specifically, not the enemy player's. A straight line between the centers of both heroes must be free of vision blockers.
Leaning around a corner only exposes the hero's torso and does not count as line of sight, meaning neither hero will reveal the other on the minimap while one is leaning.
A visual of the maximum range that the player will reveal enemy heroes (that are in line of sight) on the minimap
Being very near an enemy hero (just under light melee range), even if they are facing away. The hero will be revealed even if they are not visible on screen, as long as there are no vision blockers.
Bebop cannot see McGinnis on his minimap, and McGinnis would not be able to see Bebop on the minimap (were she looking at him).
Carrying the Urn.
Activating
Mirage
's
Djinn's Mark
on an enemy.
Things that will not reveal the player to enemies on the minimap
[
edit
|
edit source
]
Standing near enemy Troopers while they are not shooting at or targeting the player.
Using abilities or items on enemy Troopers or heroes.
Being targeted or shot at by an enemy hero from beyond approximately
50–60 meters
, even if they have line of sight on the player.
Enemy Troopers riding a
zipline
near or above the player.
Shooting at summons such as
Graves
' Ghouls or Deadheads or deployables such as
McGinnis
turrets.
Being hit by summons or deployable abilities.
Invisibility
[
edit
|
edit source
]
Invisibility (from
Veil Walker
,
Shadow Weave
, or
Haze
's
Smoke Bomb
) prevents revealing under the following rules:
Breaking invisibility requires: using abilities, shooting, or being within proximity of an enemy hero with line of sight (walls block this).
Exception:
Haze's Smoke Bomb allows using items and abilities without breaking invisibility or revealing her on the minimap.
Buildings and objectives do not break invisibility or reveal the player.
Graves' ghouls will chase invisible players but will not explode on them.
Hero Interactions
[
edit
|
edit source
]
Calico
cannot be revealed on the minimap while using
Ava
, even if shot by troopers, guardians, or walkers.
Mirage
's
Djinn's Mark
will reveal invisible enemies on the minimap when activated.
Item Interactions
[
edit
|
edit source
]
Stalker
provides wall-hack vision of the target but does not reveal them on the minimap for the team.

guides.title: Movement
guides.content:
“Alright buddy, give 'em hell!”
This page is under construction. To avoid
edit conflicts
, please consult the
edit history
and the
talk page
.
Main article:
Portal:Movement
In
Deadlock
, there are a variety of methods for traversing the map.
Overview
[
edit
|
edit source
]
Movement is a concept that encompasses traversing the
map
. In a broad sense, it is important for positioning, map rotations, ganking, and pushing, while from a mechanical point of view, it is the specific actions to escape, chase, initiate fights, or otherwise catch enemies out of position.
Several heroes and items have specific movement tech that cannot be executed otherwise.
Distances and speeds are displayed in-game by meters (m) and meters per second (m/s). As the game built on the Source 2 Engine, internal measurements are calculated by hammer units (hu), where 1 hammer unit can be approximated to 1
inch
. Therefore, 1 hu is roughly equal to and represents .0254 m.
There is no fall damage in
Deadlock
.
Basic Movement
[
edit
|
edit source
]
Controls
[
edit
|
edit source
]
The character is controlled using movement keys (by default:
W
,
A
,
S
, and
D
). Pressing the jump key (by default:
Space
) makes the character jump, and holding it near a ledge allows the character to mantle (by default:
Space
). The crouch key (default:
Ctrl
) causes the character to crouch or slide if they have enough speed  (8.9 m/s or 350 hu/s) or are on an inclined surface, such as stairs. Additionally, the dash key (default:
Shift
) enables the character to perform a
dash
both on the ground and in the air.
Heroes
have differing move speeds and
dash speeds
.
Main articles:
Move Speed
,
Movement Slow
Heroes have differing base moving and sprinting speeds. Sprinting will activate on its own, which occurs after a hero is out of combat for five seconds and persists until entering combat or zooming in. Zooming in while sprinting resets the player's speed back to base move speed. Most heroes can obtain sprint through items, but several start with it initially.
Air Jump
[
edit
|
edit source
]
Jumping while midair functions like a double jump, allowing the player to reach rooftops or get over tall walls. This consumes one stamina.
At the start of each game, you are only permitted one Air Jump per jump. This can be expanded to two with the
Vitality
Item
Stamina Mastery
.
Load video
Local File
Local File might collect personal data.
Continue
Dismiss
https://deadlock.wiki/File:Air_Jump.mp4
Air Jumping
Wall Jump
[
edit
|
edit source
]
Pressing
Space
next to a wall will cause the character to perform a Wall Jump, which does not consume stamina. You can Wall Jump multiple times per jump, but each Wall Jump after the first one costs 0.5
stamina
and incurs a fatigue that reduces the height of successive jumps. This fatigue recovers non-linearly
[
Citation needed
]
over the course of 1.25 seconds and recovers completely upon landing. Using a heavy melee while airborne will also incur fatigue.
Load video
Local File
Local File might collect personal data.
Continue
Dismiss
https://deadlock.wiki/File:Wall_Jump.mp4
Wall Jumping
Air Dash
[
edit
|
edit source
]
Dashing midair can be done similarly to a grounded dash. A dash performed in the air is called an Air Dash, and consumes the same amount of stamina as a grounded dash. You can Air Dash only once per jump, but this limit can be expanded to two with the
Vitality
Item
Stamina Mastery
.
Load video
Local File
Local File might collect personal data.
Continue
Dismiss
https://deadlock.wiki/File:Air_Dash.mp4
Air Dashing
Dash Jump
[
edit
|
edit source
]
A
Dash jump
is a fast long jump, which can be performed as an additive input during a dash during a set timing window and which costs 1
stamina
, for a total of 2 stamina. The timing of a
dash jump
is visualized by the stamina bars under your crosshair turning blue while
dashing.
While the crosshair is blue, the player can press jump to
dash jump
.
Load video
Local File
Local File might collect personal data.
Continue
Dismiss
https://deadlock.wiki/File:Dash_Jump.mp4
Dash Jumping
Down Dash
[
edit
|
edit source
]
Double tapping crouch midair performs a down dash, allowing the player to return to the ground quickly. This consumes 0.5
[
1
]
stamina. You can Down Dash only once per jump. This can be expanded to two with the
Vitality
Item
Stamina Mastery
.
Load video
Local File
Local File might collect personal data.
Continue
Dismiss
https://deadlock.wiki/File:Downdash.mp4
Down dashing
Mantle
[
edit
|
edit source
]
Mantling is the act of climbing a nearby ledge. If a player holds the crouch key (default:
Ctrl
) while mantling, they will slide after climbing up, which is called a Mantle Glide.
If the player is hit by a
melee attack
while mantling, they will get an 80%
Movement Slow
, which will taper down to 20% over 2s.
Stamina
[
edit
|
edit source
]
Main article:
Stamina
Stamina management is an important aspect of player movement.
Map features
[
edit
|
edit source
]
There are multiple elements on the map that let players move uniquely.
Jump Pads
[
edit
|
edit source
]
Main article:
Jump pad
Jump Pads are small vents that push heroes through the air after standing on them.
Ropes
[
edit
|
edit source
]
Main article:
Ropes
Ropes dangle from the roofs of buildings or inside buildings, allowing heroes to access rooftops or additional floors, and facilitate vertical traversal.
Teleporter
[
edit
|
edit source
]
Main article:
Teleporter
There are multiple pairs of teleporters throughout the map that can be entered to quickly traverse left and right across the map.
Ziplines
[
edit
|
edit source
]
Main article:
Ziplines
Ziplines are the most prominent method of traversal, as the heroes spawn riding the lines. They connect the opposing bases together and facilitate fights.
Advanced Movement
[
edit
|
edit source
]
As a physics based game, several principles in the real world are also modeled in-game. However, quirks with the game engine allow unique movement options that are not readily apparent.
Melee Jump
[
edit
|
edit source
]
Melee jumping is the technique of using a heavy melee timed with a
Wall Jump
to go up without double jumping, thus saving a Stamina bar.
Load video
Local File
Local File might collect personal data.
Continue
Dismiss
https://deadlock.wiki/File:Melee_Jump.mp4
Melee Jumping and then
Mantling
Corner Boost
[
edit
|
edit source
]
Corner boosting
is a technique that exploits the game's wall jump mechanics to achieve greater vertical velocity than a standard wall jump. The effect is believed to occur due to the following implementation behavior:
Velocity Reset on Wall Jump Initiation
– When a wall jump is performed, the game first sets the character's velocity to a fixed absolute value.
Collision Normal Calculation
– The game determines the direction of the jump by using the normal vector of the character's collision capsule at the nearest point of contact with the wall.
Upward-Angled Normals on Corners
– Because the collision capsule is rounded, the normal vector near a corner can point diagonally upward or downward rather than purely sideways. When the normal is angled upward, the resulting velocity is applied at a steeper angle, leading to increased upward momentum.
Visual Comparison
[
edit
|
edit source
]
Normal Wall Jump – Final velocity is directed primarily sideways, following a standard wall jump.
Corner Boost – Final velocity is angled higher due to the upward-tilting normal, resulting in a stronger ascent.
Load video
Local File
Local File might collect personal data.
Continue
Dismiss
https://deadlock.wiki/File:Corner_Boost.mp4
Corner Boosting
Dropdown Corner Boost
[
edit
|
edit source
]
Same as
Corner Boost
,
Dropdown Corner Boost
can be done on any edge in the game. To perform it, drop down from an edge and wall jump to gain extra height without using stamina.
Load video
Local File
Local File might collect personal data.
Continue
Dismiss
https://deadlock.wiki/File:Dropdown_Corner_Boost.mp4
Dropdown Corner Boosting
Friction
[
edit
|
edit source
]
“Alright buddy, give 'em hell!”
This page is under construction. To avoid
edit conflicts
, please consult the
edit history
and the
talk page
.
Dash Jump Cancel
[
edit
|
edit source
]
Dash Jump Canceling is a technique that allows for Jumping immediately after a Dash without performing a Dash Jump. After performing a Dash for a short duration it is possible to spend 1
Stamina
to perform a Dash Jump. Casting an ability during the Dash Jump window will immediately cancel it. This can then be followed up with a normal Jump. When done correctly a Dash Jump Cancel can be used to travel further than a Dash.
Only some abilities provide a benefit when used to Dash Jump Cancel. Abilities with long cast time or abilities that slow movement during cast provide almost no benefit.
Movement abilities
[
edit
|
edit source
]
Certain abilities are classified in-game as Movement Abilties. These abilities are disabled by the
Spirit
Item
Slowing Hex
.
Trivia
[
edit
|
edit source
]
Prior to the
April 17, 2025 update
, players could use
Heavy Melee Cancel
to maintain momentum from a Heavy Melee attack. However, the update significantly reduced the momentum preservation, making the technique nearly ineffective for movement purposes.
↑
https://forums.playdeadlock.com/threads/12-29-2025-update.95233/#post-185824

guides.title: Powerups
guides.content:
Two Powerup stones in Sandbox mode
Powerup duration icons (all 4 active at once)
Powerups
, also known as
Bridge Buffs
or
Runes
, are temporary buffs that spawn as a glowing stone on the bridges connecting two buildings near the
Urn
spawn and dropoff points, starting at 5 minutes and then every 5 minutes thereafter. They require a Heavy
Melee Attack
to claim.
Powerups are displayed on the
Minimap
with unique icons indicating their type.
Powerups start weaker and grow stronger (from 5 minutes to 40 minutes). Exact values are the following:
Casting:
Starts at +15
Spirit Power
and +12%
Cooldown Reduction
for 160s
Ends at +65 Spirit Power and +20% Cooldown Reduction for 160s
Gun:
Starts at +12%
Fire Rate
and +35%
Ammo
for 160s
Ends at +35% Fire Rate and +70% Ammo for 160s
Movement:
Starts at +2
Stamina
, +1.5m/s
Sprint Speed
, +20% Stamina Regeneration Rate, and +40% Zipline Boost for 160s
Ends at +4 Stamina, +4m/s Sprint Speed, +50% Stamina Regeneration Rate and +80% Zipline Boost for 160s
Survival:
Starts at +200
Bonus Health
and +4 (fixed)
Health Regen
for 160s
Ends at +700 Bonus Health and +40 (fixed)
Health Regen
for 160s
These temporary stat increases do not appear in the in-game stats, but they are listed as active effects on the left side of the player's HUD. The duration of powerup effects appears in the lower left of the HUD, above the soul count. When another player has the powerup, the icon is shown below their healthbar.

guides.title: Ranked mode
guides.content:
“Dead.”
This page contains game content that has been removed from the game.
In-game description of Ranked mode
For the current rank system, see
Skill Rating
.
“
Ranked is a challenge mode that provides a more competitive Deadlock experience where you'll be matchmade with players of similar skill to determine your Skill Rank among the global population.
Ranked mode
was a game mode in Deadlock for competitive players to compete with other players of similar skill levels. By winning matches and performing well, players could earn badges for their profile depending on the highest rank they achieve.
Medals were updated once per week, requiring at least 7 games played within that week to receive a rank.
[
1
]
Ranked mode was added to the game on
October 10, 2024
, but was not publicly available until October 15th. Medals were granted the following Tuesday, on October 22nd. The gamemode was removed on November 21st. Ranks still appears in a player's profile as
Skill Ratings
.
Overview
[
edit
|
edit source
]
The rank badge can be found by viewing the profile. In ranked mode, an additional small icon was displayed when hovering other player's profiles and on the hero's icon in the end game stats.
In order to play, players:
Had to have 50 games played
Had to have zero behavior-related restrictions (Voice/text bans, report misuse, etc.)
Ranks
[
edit
|
edit source
]
Players were grouped into the below ranks, with Eternus as the highest and most skilled rank available.
Within each rank, players were further assigned a subrank from I to VI, with VI the highest subrank. The ranks, from lowest to highest were:

guides.title: Rejuvenator
guides.content:
The Rejuvenator.
The
Rejuvenator
is a single rune that spawns above the
Mid-Boss
pit after it is slain. It appears as a large, green crystal. After the Mid-Boss is killed, the Rejuvenator descends over 6 seconds and cannot be claimed until it fully touches the ground. Once it lands, any player in the match can hit it with a heavy
melee attack
, granting the
Rejuvenation Credit
buff to their entire team. Each heavy melee hit grants one Rejuvenator Credit to the team, up to 3. Hitting the Rejuvenator also heals the first player who punched it to full health.
Rejuvenation Credit
[
edit
|
edit source
]
The Rejuvenation Credit is a team-wide buff granted to all allies when a Rejuvenator crystal is claimed. The buff lasts 4 minutes or until all credits are consumed, whichever comes first.
When there are 30 seconds left on the buff duration, Heroes are warned that it will expire soon.
The buff allows each player to revive with
100%
of their health in the place of their death after
3
seconds. Upon death, the player respawns in place, loses the buff, and their team's Rejuvenator Credits are reduced by 1 (each player can only consume one credit). The top UI bar displays the remaining Rejuvenator Credits, and players who haven't consumed a credit have a highlight below their net worth. When all credits are used, the buff is removed from all team members.
Killing a player with a Rejuvenator credit does not appear as a kill in the HUD and does not grant any
souls
, but it does trigger "on hero kill" effects such as the cooldown reset of
Shiv
's
Killing Blow
or the
stack
buildup of
Grey Talon
's,
Mo & Krill
's and
Vindicta
's ultimates.
Update history
[
edit
|
edit source
]
See all updates:
Rejuvenator Update history
Gallery
[
edit
|
edit source
]
Rejuvenator
Minimap
icon
Trivia
[
edit
|
edit source
]
Billy
makes a reference to the Rejuvenator rework, saying that "it used to go out to everybody until
the Man
decided it was too good".
[
1
]
References

edit
|
edit source
]

's in-game
voice line
(picked up Rejuv) -
"You know, this used to go to everybody 'til the Man decided it was too good."

guides.title: Ropes
guides.content:
A rope on Greenwich
Ropes are climbable entities in the game that can be grabbed onto by pressing the jump key. If a player is hit by a melee attack while on a rope, they are
slowed
by 80%, which ramps down to 20% over 2s.
If a
Hero
has taken damage from another Hero in the last 4s, they will climb ropes 40% slower, which tapers off to 0% in the last second.
Ropes are often located next to buildings that have
Jungle camps
on their rooftops.

guides.title: Seasonal events
guides.content:
“
The New York Oracle wants to wish you and your loved ones a safe Maelstrom, and a joyous winter!
— The
Newscaster
Seasonal events
are temporary events in
Deadlock
to celebrate holidays.
Halloween
[
edit
|
edit source
]
The 2024 Halloween event ran from October 31 to November 7. The 2025 Halloween event ran from October 24 to November 12.
It added 4 Halloween masks for heroes and pumpkin models for boxes, statues and
Denizens
. The Halloween masks were randomized.
Gallery
[
edit
|
edit source
]
Halloween masks
Heroes wearing Halloween masks
Heroes wearing Halloween masks
Halloween Jungle
Denizens
Winter
[
edit
|
edit source
]
2025
[
edit
|
edit source
]
Winter 2025 souvenir
The 2025 Winter event ran from December 15 to January 20.
During the event, all players received a unique
Snowball
item that could be leveled up by winning matches with different Heroes, which also unlocked ornaments for the Christmas tree in the
Hideout
. Players who participated in the Winter challenge also unlocked a souvenir above their fireplace in the
Hideout
.
The
Shopkeeper
also received new holiday-themed lines for all heroes, and
Drifter
has holiday-themed songs when using Eternal Night.
2024
[
edit
|
edit source
]
2024 Winter main menu
The 2024 Winter event ran from December 17 to January 17.
It added a new main menu animation, remixed music for the menu and shops, and Winter-themed decorations in the streets, buildings, and
Curiosity Shop
. The
Shopkeeper
featured a Santa mask and unique lines. In addition, the
Denizens
were modelled after snowmen.
Dynamo
and the
Newscaster
had unique holiday voice lines.
All the mainline
Heroes
received unique Winter skins, which could be unlocked by completing their respective challenge. A list of which skins were unlocked during the event can be seen in the "Showcase" tab in the player's profile, next to the "Match History" button. Players who participated in the Winter challenge also unlocked a snow globe above their fireplace in the
Hideout
.
Skin challenges
[
edit
|
edit source
]
As the winter skin challenges weren't listed in-game or in patch notes, the challenges were discovered and shared by the player community over the duration of the event.
These challenges could be achieved only in a normal match or a labs match, not in a custom game or against bots. The conditions for each challenge must be completed in
one match.
Winter skins were tracked in-game by a Santa hat icon in the main menu that showed how many skins had already been unlocked. Heroes with Winter skins had the Santa hat icon and a red background in the Heroes page.
The skins can be tested in sandbox mode or custom games (with cheats enabled) with the
console commands
ent_fire player setbodygroup hat,1
and
ent_fire player setbodygroup xmas,1
.
*Note: Vindicta's challenge can also be completed by killing an enemy while flying and then getting killed mid-flight, as the game seems to count the player's death as a kill.
[
1
]
Getting killed may also work for Seven's challenge.
Gallery
[
edit
|
edit source
]

April Fools
[
edit
|
edit source
]
The 2026 April Fools day event enabled multiples of the same hero in a match.
Trivia
[
edit
|
edit source
]
The Winter
Curiosity Shop
has a list of all the heroes who have been "nice" or "naughty". Only
Lash
is marked as naughty.

guides.title: Sinner's Sacrifice
guides.content:
Sinner's Sacrifice in-game
Sinner's Sacrifice
machines are
breakable
objects in the
map
that may be broken using a
melee attack
to earn
Souls
and other rewards.
Locations
[
edit
|
edit source
]
There are a total of 10 Sinner's Sacrifice locations and 3 types of sites on the map.
6 sites of the machine by itself
2 "hybrid" camps of 1 machine and 2
Medium Denizens
2 "hybrid" camps of 2 machines and 1 Medium Denizen
They spawn
8
minutes after game starts and respawn
5
minutes after being broken (solo) or fully cleared ("hybrid").
Sites are shown on the map with a
icon (a rectangle with a circle within it). It should be noted that the icon represents the site and not the Sinner's Sacrifice machine. The icon of a "hybrid" camp will not disappear from the map after breaking the machine but instead when fully cleared.
Behavior
[
edit
|
edit source
]
Interacting with the machine
[
edit
|
edit source
]
https://deadlock.wiki/File:Vault_music.mp3
Music that plays on loop from the Sinner's Sacrifice
Each machine has 500 health and can only be damaged by
melee attacks
. A light melee deals 50 damage, and a heavy melee deals 100 damage.
The machine retaliates against attackers by dealing 80 damage back per a melee hit with the total retaliation damage to a hero being 320-640 depending on the mixture weights of melee strikes: Primarily eight light melee strikes 8 x 80 = 640 retaliation damage vs. four heavy melee strikes 4 x 80 = 320 retaliation damage. There is virtually no time difference between light or heavy melee exclusivity.
Abilities that deal melee damage, such as
Viscous
'
Puddle Punch
or
Calico
's
Leaping Slash
, will not affect the machine.
The machine's health is indicated by the lightbulbs at the top of the machine: The first melee attack will cause all the lights to turn on and further hits will cause them to turn off from right to left. A light melee will turn off one bulb per hit and a heavy melee turns off two bulbs.
Jackpot
[
edit
|
edit source
]
https://deadlock.wiki/File:Vault_music_hit_05.mp3
Jackpot fanfare music
Ideal jackpot timing for a heavy melee last hit
After being reduced to one or zero bulbs (400 damage taken), the Sinner's Sacrifice will enter a looping
3
-second timing minigame. The lights will begin turning on from left to right accompanied by a count of 8 sound effects.
Hitting the machine within the final
1.3
-second window when all the bulbs are on will cause a "Jackpot," represented by the last hit dealing 100 damage regardless of melee type, fanfare playing, and the slot rolls landing on three red eyes. If the Jackpot timing is missed, the player receives the same soul reward as a regular final hit.
Retaliation damage is deactivated during this state.
Rewards
[
edit
|
edit source
]
Souls
[
edit
|
edit source
]
Each Sinner's Sacrifice internally holds a total bounty value that increases over the course of a match. This value starts at
310
and grows by
3.35
per minute from the beginning of the match.
The table below shows its approximate total worth at key intervals.
This total value is distributed in
Unsecured Souls
with each hit:
A
light melee
grants the total value divided by 20, rounded.
A
heavy melee
grants the total value divided by 10, rounded.
The
final hit
grants the total value divided by 2 (rounded), plus one heavy melee payout.
A
Jackpot
adds 1 bonus soul to the final hit payout.
The payout is intended to grant half of its souls from melee attacks and the other half when destroyed. However, due to rounding on each hit, the total souls granted may be slightly more or less than the internal value shown above.
If multiple people hit the machine, subsequent melee attacks will attempt to evenly distribute the payout to everyone that has hit it. If there is a remainder after the division, the most recent attacker receives the extra soul.
Buffs
[
edit
|
edit source
]
Successfully hitting the Jackpot provides the player with stacks of the
Golden Statue
buff.
Hitting the jackpot with a
light melee
gives the player 1 buff.
Hitting with a
heavy melee
gives 4 buffs.
To get the jackpot with a heavy melee last hit, the heavy melee needs to be charged before the 2nd-to-last lightbulb lights up.
Update history
[
edit
|
edit source
]
See all updates:
Sinner's Sacrifice Update history
Trivia
[
edit
|
edit source
]
In
Neon Prime
and early
Deadlock,
Sinner's Sacrifices were originally designed as
Vaults
, and didn't have the jackpot mechanic.

guides.title: Skill Rating
guides.content:
In-game description of Skill Rating.
Skill Rating
or
Skill Ranks
is a system in
Deadlock
that determines a player's matchmaking rating and hero-based rating.
It was added in the
November 21, 2024 Update
and replaced
Ranked mode
.
Overview
[
edit
|
edit source
]
Every player starts with the provisional rating of Obscurus. Once a player has played enough Normal mode matches, they will be assigned a new rating. Only Normal mode matches counts for skill rating. The rating is automatically updated based on the player's performance after every Normal match.
Matches in which a player has disconnected within the first 5 minutes do not count for skill rating.
A player's rating is less affected by "bad quality" matches with large skill disparity, and more affected by evenly balanced matches.
The player can enable an option in settings to see the average rating of each
Team
in a match in the post-game screen, as well as an option to be notified when their rating increases.
Hero-Based Rating
[
edit
|
edit source
]
The Skill Rating tab also shows the player's hero-based rating, which represents their skill performance with each hero relative to their core rating. It is represented by one or two green upward arrows and one or two gray downward arrows. Green arrows and gray arrows indicate if the player's matchmaking rating with that hero is high or low relative to their core skill rating, respectively. Unplayed heroes start with two gray arrows.
Leaderboards
[
edit
|
edit source
]
The Global leaderboard showcases the top 1,000
Deadlock
players per region, as well as up to 3 of their highest-rated heroes. Hero leaderboards showcase the top 1.000 players of each hero available in Normal mode, per region.
A player needs to have played a minimum of 50 matches in the past 30 days to appear in the global leaderboard, and a minimum of 20 matches with a specific hero in the past 30 days to appear in that hero's leaderboard.
The leaderboard regions are North America, South America, Europe, Asia and Oceania.
Skill Ratings
[
edit
|
edit source
]
Players are assigned into the below ratings, with Eternus as the highest and most skilled rating available.
Within each rating, players are further assigned a subrating from I to VI, with VI the highest subrating. The ratings, from lowest to highest are:
†All players start as Obscurus before they receive their rating.
Initiate, Rating 1
[
edit
|
edit source
]
Seeker, Rating 2
[
edit
|
edit source
]
Alchemist, Rating 3
[
edit
|
edit source
]
Arcanist, Rating 4
[
edit
|
edit source
]
Ritualist, Rating 5
[
edit
|
edit source
]
Emissary, Rating 6
[
edit
|
edit source
]
Archon, Rating 7
[
edit
|
edit source
]
Oracle, Rating 8
[
edit
|
edit source
]
Phantom, Rating 9
[
edit
|
edit source
]
Ascendant, Rating 10
[
edit
|
edit source
]
Eternus, Rating 11
[
edit
|
edit source
]
Update history
[
edit
|
edit source
]

guides.title: Soul Urn
guides.content:
“
You'd think people would be more excited about a jar of souls!
— Soul Urn
The
Soul Urn
, also known as
Spirit Urn, Soul Jar
or
Spirit Jar
, is an objective that spawns at the 10-minute mark and respawns in 5-minute intervals.
Players can pick up the urn and deliver it to its destination on the opposite side of the map. Doing so grants the delivering team
Soul Orbs
, a
Golden statue
buff, and additional souls for the courier.
Soul Urn icon
Carrying the soul urn
[
edit
|
edit source
]
After 10 minutes, the Soul Urn spawns in one of two locations in the outermost lanes: beside the overhanging bridge at York Street (Yellow Lane) or beside the bridge at Park Avenue (Green Lane). The urn always spawns first on Yellow Lane at 10 minutes and alternates lanes every 5 minutes. It descends from the sky for 12 seconds before it can be picked up. A player can grab the landed urn by standing near it for 2 seconds. Being
melee
'd or
stunned
while picking up the urn resets its channel time, and the player cannot
parry
while picking it up.
While carrying the urn, the courier is affected in several ways:
They get a
Move Speed
bonus of +3.5 m/s (capped at 15 m/s max), get their
Sprint Speed
disabled and have 100%
Slow Resistance
and 30%
Bullet
and
Spirit Resist
.
They are
disarmed
,
silenced
, and cannot use active items.
Channeled abilities and items started before picking up the urn, like
Ivy
's
Air Drop
and
Magic Carpet
, are not interrupted. Abilities can still be activated for about half a second while picking up urn.
Viscous
cannot carry as
Goo Ball
.
While Ivy can no longer pick up the urn while activating her ult, she can still fly allies who are carrying it, or pick the urn up during ult if she stays over it while flying. The latter can be achieved by dropping the urn inside a room with a low roof to fly inside.
They are revealed to the enemy team on the
Minimap
.
They cannot use
Ziplines
or
Teleporters
.
Going through
the Doorman
's
Doors
drops the urn at the entrance door.
Rem
and
Calico
(during
Ava
) cannot enter
vents
.
The courier can drop the urn at any time by pressing their
parry
key. A
melee attack
(light or heavy) or any
stun
from an enemy (including
Troopers
) will also force the courier to drop it. If the urn is left on the ground for too long, it will walk back to its spawn point.
Note:
The Soul Urn's soul value is determined at the moment it spawns (e.g., 10:00, 15:00). Holding onto the urn for longer durations does not increase its value.
Drop-off location
[
edit
|
edit source
]
Green candles indicating favored drop-off on the player's side
The drop-off point is on the opposite side of the map, marked by a Soul Urn outline icon on the Minimap and colored red if an enemy player is currently carrying the urn. The urn is instantly turned in when the courier steps onto the drop-off (being in the air doesn't count).
If a team is behind by a certain soul threshold (15% for the first urn, 10% after), the drop-off point moves closer to their side. This "favored" status is indicated by four floating green candles around the urn, and a green arrow over the urn pointing to the favored side in the minimap. For the leading team (drop-off on the enemy's side), the candles and minimap arrow are red.
The drop-off location is determined when an urn is first picked up by any player. Once determined, the drop-off point is fixed until the next urn spawn, even if the urn walks back to its spawn or the team soul difference changes.
Anti-stalling mechanics
[
edit
|
edit source
]
To prevent one team from holding the urn for too long without delivering it, the urn begins to damage its courier for 1.5% of their max health per second and disables their
Health Regen
after carrying it for 45 seconds. Furthermore, if the urn is dropped after that point the courier's entire team is prevented from picking up the urn again for 12 seconds. The 45-second timer pauses if the urn is dropped and resets if the enemy team picks it up.
Soul urn reward
[
edit
|
edit source
]
The Soul Urn's base soul bounty is
1300 Souls
and increases by
230 Souls per minute
of game time. This value is "locked in" at the minute the Urn spawns.
10-minute Urn:
3,600 Souls
20-minute Urn:
5,900 Souls
30-minute Urn:
8,200 Souls
When a team successfully turns in the Soul Urn:
A random
Golden Statue buff
is granted to each member of the capturing team.
50% of the base value is distributed
instantly and equally
to all team members.
50% of the base value is spawned as contestable
Soul Orbs
.
Courier's bonus
[
edit
|
edit source
]
The courier receives a personal bonus equal to
35% of their total share
(Base Share + Comeback Bonus). This bonus is awarded
instantly
upon delivery and cannot be denied by the enemy team. They also get 4 random
Golden Statue
buffs.
The comeback mechanic
[
edit
|
edit source
]
“If we are to complete the ritual, we must think clearly.”
This page may contain
factual inaccuracies
. The information presented requires verification against reliable sources.
Issue:
The math for comeback bounty may be inaccurate and needs more testing
If the capturing team is behind by
10% or more
(15% for the first urn), the Urn becomes a Comeback Urn. This adds a
Comeback Bonus
to the reward:
Bonus Calculation:
The bonus scales based on game time and the team's soul deficit. It uses a growth rate of
130 Souls per minute
.
Approximate Formula:
Individual Bonus = 130 × Minutes × Deficit %
Safe Payout:
The entire Comeback Bonus is added to the
Instant Soul
pool.
Orbs:
The contestable Soul Orbs
do not increase
during a comeback; they remain fixed at 50% of the original base share.
Note:
The first 3,000 souls of net worth difference are generally ignored when calculating the deficit, which may slightly dampen the bonus for smaller leads.
Reward scenarios
[
edit
|
edit source
]
The following examples use the
10-minute Soul Urn
(Base Value: 3,600 Souls).
Scenario 1: Standard delivery (Team is winning or even)
[
edit
|
edit source
]
Individual Base Share:
600 Souls (3,600 ÷ 6)
Orbs per player:
300 Souls (50% of share, deniable)
Instant per player:
300 Souls (50% of share, guaranteed)
Each Teammate receives:
600 Souls total (300 instant + 300 from orbs)
The Courier receives:
810 Souls total (300 instant + 300 orbs + 210 courier bonus)
The courier's 35% bonus is: 600 × 0.35 = 210 souls.
Scenario 2: Comeback delivery (30% deficit)
[
edit
|
edit source
]
Individual Base Share:
600 Souls
Comeback Bonus per player:
130 × 10 × 0.30 = 390 Souls
Total Individual Share:
990 Souls (600 base + 390 bonus)
Each Teammate receives:
990 Souls total (690 instant + 300 from orbs)
Instant souls include 300 (base) + 390 (comeback).
The Courier receives:
1,337 Souls total (1,037 instant + 300 orbs)
Courier bonus is: 990 × 0.35 = 346.5 souls.
Instant souls include 300 (base) + 390 (comeback) + 346.5 (bonus).
Note:
Team Instant
refers to the total non-deniable souls granted to the team (Instant shares + Courier bonus). All fractional soul values have been rounded.
Update history
[
edit
|
edit source
]
See all updates:
Soul Urn Update history
Lore
[
edit
|
edit source
]
Trapper
builds Soul Urns for the New York Municipal Coven.
[
1
]
The Urn is sentient and says its job is to keep New York clean by capturing rogue spirits, which should then be released in another location.
The Urn nags heroes to be delivered quickly and believes its work is underappreciated.
Trivia
[
edit
|
edit source
]
In
Neon Prime
, the Urn was called the
Idol
and dropped off at the Center Temple, a location above the
Mid-Boss
.
See also
[
edit
|
edit source
]
Soul Urn/Quotes
References
[
edit
|
edit source
]
↑
Trapper
's background -
"An employee of the New York Municipal Coven; Trapper spends his days building Spirit Urns, luring giant rats, and capturing various invasive species from The Outer Planes."

guides.title: Souls
guides.content:
Soul released by the trooper.
Dark and light soul orb. Only light orbs can be claimed or denied
Souls
are a crucial element in
Deadlock
, serving both as currency and experience points (XP) for heroes. Players collect souls by various means, including shooting
Soul Orbs
released when certain entities die, taking
Guardians
/
Walkers
, killing enemy players, and finding
Unsecured Souls
in boxes or crates. Unsecured souls are added to your total immediately but can drop upon death for other players to collect.
Every player starts the game with
600
.
Soul Orbs
[
edit
|
edit source
]
Troopers
and the
Soul Urn
release souls in
Soul Orbs
. These orbs may be shot or hit with
melee
to confirm (or deny) the souls and grant them to the attacker. If they are not attacked, then the souls will be granted to the killer automatically after around 1 second. Certain abilities like
Serrated Knives
or
Malice
can also confirm or deny orbs.
Troopers drop two orbs on death, one that floats and one that falls on the ground. The floating orb has 40% of the kill bounty and the ground orb has 60%. The ground orb cannot be denied (they appear as white to enemies) and remains for a few seconds before disappearing (their duration increases over time).
The floating soul orbs spawn dark-colored, and can only be shot and confirmed once they light up. Ally orbs are green and enemy orbs are orange. Hitting an enemy orb will deny the souls to the killer and grant them to the player who hit it. If the orb "pops" without being claimed, it's automatically rewarded to nearby allies, and if there are no allies nearby the souls are lost. Orbs can be claimed by a single bullet or pellet, regardless of damage. The bullet that hit the orb is refunded to the player's magazine.
Soul confirmation accounts for latency, which in some situations can cause the player to lose the orb even if they visually seem to have hit it first. The ally team has confirm advantage.
Soul orbs can be divided into "local" and "global" types:
Local
Orbs spawned by killing
Troopers
grant souls to the player who confirmed them and any nearby allies.
Global
Orbs spawned by delivering the
Soul Urn
grant souls to the entire team of the player who claims them.
Local Soul Sharing
[
edit
|
edit source
]
When souls are to be shared, the game checks two distances to add allied heroes to the "pool" of heroes to split the received souls:
45m from the soul orb itself when it is broken
30m from the hero that killed the Trooper
If an allied hero is within
either
of these distances, souls are shared between the killer and those allied heroes.
Soul Well
[
edit
|
edit source
]
Inside each team's base, there is a soul well that releases orbs worth 10 souls each. Unlike regular orbs, these orbs only grant souls if they are confirmed, and they can be either green or orange-colored.
Gathering Souls
[
edit
|
edit source
]
Teams with fewer souls will gain more souls from all sources (see
Comeback Mechanics
below).
Soul Distribution
[
edit
|
edit source
]
Hero
kill souls are distributed between nearby players with the following rate:
125%/57%/28%/17%/11%/8% (for 1/2/3/4/5/6 players)
EX: 1000 souls between 4 players would grant each player 170 souls (1000 * 17%)
Additionally, for kills with two or more players, the player who got the kill receives bonus souls.
Trooper
souls are distributed between nearby players with the following rate:
100%/54%/36%/25%/20%/16% (for 1/2/3/4/5/6 players)
EX: 120 souls between 4 players would grant each player 30 souls (120 * 25%)
Before the
2025-04-17
update, two players in the same lane both received 100% of souls before 8 minutes (soul duplication). Now the soul distribution is the same for the entire match, so both players get 54% per Trooper.
When a Trooper is killed, 60% of the Trooper's souls drop as a ground orb that flies to allies within 18m and remains on the ground for 18 seconds (scaling to 40 seconds after 10 minutes), while the remaining 40% fly out as a deniable soul orb that enemies can shoot. Both orbs use Soul distribution among nearby allies; enemy ground orbs are visible but cannot be claimed. Killing the Trooper with a
melee attack
grants 100% of the souls instantly, distributed among nearby allies.
Denizens (Jungle Creeps)
[
edit
|
edit source
]
Jungle camps contain
Denizens
that give souls to players on kill. They do not release soul orbs.
In order for Denizens to give souls to both players, both players must deal damage to the Denizen.
Souls from Denizens are split between all allied players who dealt damage to said Denizens.
Souls from Denizens are
unsecured
souls.
Sinner's Sacrifices
[
edit
|
edit source
]
Some jungle camps contain one or two
Sinner's Sacrifice
machines, which grants souls to the player by hitting them with
melee attacks
. Those souls are unsecured.
Breakables
[
edit
|
edit source
]
Breaking the wooden crates scattered throughout the map will have a chance to spawn some souls.
Chance for any given crate to drop souls when broken is approximately 60%
Amount of souls dropped is a function of in-game time:
[Souls Dropped] = 23 + 2.0*[Game Time in Minutes]
For example: At 30:00 game time, a broken crate will drop 83 souls.
Other
[
edit
|
edit source
]
Trophy Collector
is an item that gives the player passive souls per minute.
Killing cockroaches in underground tunnels grants the player 1 soul each.
Unsecured Souls
[
edit
|
edit source
]
Unsecured souls icon
Souls earned from killing Denizens and destroying Sinner's Sacrifice machines are
unsecured
and are lost on death. If a player dies with at least 150 unsecured souls they will be dropped in a
Soul Container
, which can be claimed by any player by hitting it with a
Heavy Melee
. Soul Containers will despawn after 3 minutes. These containers are marked with a circle icon on the
Minimap
and have a green glow effect. If a player dies with fewer than 150 unsecured souls, no Soul Container will drop—they are permanently lost.
Unsecured souls are converted into secured souls over time, meaning they will no longer be dropped on death. This conversion rate is dynamic:
Percentage Drain:
Each second,
0.5%
of the player's
current
unsecured souls are converted. This means larger amounts of unsecured souls secure faster initially.
Base Drain:
In addition to the percentage, a flat amount is secured every second. This starts at
1.6 souls/second
and increases as the match progresses (scaling with the global bounty growth of ~8% per minute).
Example at 10 Minutes:
A player carrying
1,000 unsecured souls
will secure them at a rate of roughly
7.9 souls/second
(5 from percentage + ~2.9 base).
Example at 20 Minutes:
That same player carrying
1,000 unsecured souls
will secure them faster, at a rate of roughly
9.2 souls/second
(5 from percentage + ~4.2 base), due to the base rate scaling over time.
Souls UI for Heroes
Unsecured souls may be spent in the shop, but are spent
after
secured souls. They are indicated by the silver-green ball on the Hero's hip known internally as a Soul Container, as well as in the number on the HUD with a red icon.
On the character UI, the large souls number is the
total
number of secured and unsecured souls added together.
Leveling Up
[
edit
|
edit source
]
Gathering souls will gradually "level up" your hero, granting a variety of benefits. See
boons
for detailed information.
Soul Values
[
edit
|
edit source
]
Soul Value:
Value of the target at the time = 0m
Soul Gain:
How the value changes over the time of the match, measured in minutes
* Soul gain from Urns is divided among the team. The Courier gets slightly more souls.
** Soul gain from structures is split with 40% given to nearby allied heroes, while 60% is divided amongst the entire team.
First Blood bonus
[
edit
|
edit source
]
A player gets 150 bonus souls on their first kill.
Comeback Mechanics
[
edit
|
edit source
]
When a team is behind on souls, there are some "comeback mechanics" that come into play to allow the losing team to quickly catch up.
Killing an enemy hero will award more souls if that hero is stronger than the killer team's average networth, regardless of which team is leading in souls.
The Soul Urn will give 130% additional souls to a team that is behind.
The losing team gains up to 30% more souls from Troopers, Denizens, Sinner's Sacrifices and Objectives, based on how behind they are (peaks at 20% max net worth difference). The first 3k in net worth difference is ignored.
Catch up Mechanics
[
edit
|
edit source
]
Catch up mechanics allow for players with low net worth to catch up to the other players on their team.
The two players on each team with the lowest net worth have a passive gain of souls per second based on the total souls per second gathered by the team (2.5% and 1.5% for the lowest and second-lowest player, respectively). This occurs after laning phase (8 min).
For example, if a team gathered 200 souls in the last second, the lowest player earns 5 extra souls.
Update history
[
edit
|
edit source
